question.text: Karthik invests Rs. 1,00,000 of his own funds in a debt instrument yielding 12% per annum. He uses 1.5x leverage (i.e., total investment = 1.5 times his own funds) by borrowing the additional amount at 9% per annum. What is Karthik's Return on Equity (ROE)?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Total investment = Rs. 1,00,000 x 1.5 = Rs. 1,50,000. Borrowed amount = Rs. 1,50,000 - Rs. 1,00,000 = Rs. 50,000. Gross return = Rs. 1,50,000 x 12% = Rs. 18,000. Interest on borrowed funds = Rs. 50,000 x 9% = Rs. 4,500. Net return = Rs. 18,000 - Rs. 4,500 = Rs. 13,500. ROE = Rs. 13,500 / Rs. 1,00,000 = 13.5%. Leverage enhanced the base 12% return to 13.5% because the spread between asset return (12%) and borrowing cost (9%) is positive. **The Trap:** 12% ignores the leverage effect entirely. 15% incorrectly calculates by adding the full 3% spread to 12%. 10.5% mistakenly deducts borrowing cost from total return without proper computation. **Cross-Exam Context:** Leverage and its impact on ROE is tested in NISM X-B (Case Studies chapter) and Series VIII (Equity Derivatives). Understanding that leverage amplifies both gains and losses is crucial. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** While leverage boosts ROE when asset returns exceed borrowing cost, the reverse is devastating. If the asset returned only 6%, ROE would be (6% x 1.5L - 9% x 0.5L) / 1L = (9000 - 4500) / 100000 = 4.5% — less than the unleveraged 6%. Always explain leverage as a double-edged sword.
